facts_disclosed: Planning to stage at Noma in Copenhagen — applied for a two-week stage

turns.content: I can't sleep. It's almost 2am and I just sent an email that might change my entire life and now I'm lying here staring at the ceiling like an idiot
That sounds intense. What kind of email keeps you up at 2am staring at the ceiling?
I applied to do a stage. Like a professional kitchen stage — you go work in someone else's restaurant for free, basically. To learn. It's a big deal in the culinary world
I know what a stage is — that's a serious step. Which restaurant did you apply to?
Noma. In Copenhagen. I actually typed it out and hit send and now I feel like I'm going to throw up
Noma is about as high as it gets. That's a bold move. What made you decide to go for it now?

Honestly? I've been thinking about it for years. Rene Redzepi is the reason I became a chef. Not Babushka — she's the reason I learned to cook, that's different. But Redzepi made me see that a kitchen could be a laboratory. That food could be... I don't know, revolutionary. I watched every interview, read his journal, studied every menu. And I kept saying "one day" and one day never came so tonight I just did it
There's a meaningful distinction there — learning to cook versus choosing it as your life's work. What does the stage involve exactly? How long would you be there?

Two weeks. That's the standard stage they offer. Two weeks in Copenhagen working in that kitchen. Mon Dieu, just thinking about it — the foraging program, the fermentation lab, the way they think about Nordic ingredients. It would be like going to church if I believed in God
Two weeks of full immersion in that environment could be transformative. Have you thought about the logistics — time off from your current position, travel, accommodation in Copenhagen?

Putain, don't start with logistics, I just want to live in the fantasy for five minutes. But yes, okay. Chef Antoine would need to give me the time. Copenhagen is expensive. I'd probably eat ramen in a hostel for two weeks. I don't care. I'd sleep on the floor of that kitchen if they let me
Fair enough — the logistics can wait until you actually hear back. For now, you put yourself out there, and that took guts. How selective is their stage program?

Very. They get hundreds of applications. Most people who stage there are already head chefs or have Michelin experience. I'm a sous chef at a restaurant in Montreal that most people outside Quebec have never heard of. But my letter was good. I talked about growing up between Russian and French food traditions, about how displacement shaped the way I think about terroir. I think it was honest and that counts for something

That angle is genuinely compelling — the intersection of Russian and French culinary traditions filtered through the experience of immigration is exactly the kind of perspective a place like Noma values. They're not just looking for technique, they're looking for people who think differently about food. When would you expect to hear back?

Could be weeks. Could be months. Could be never — they might just not respond. That's the worst part. But I did it. I pressed send. Whatever happens, I did it. Okay I need to actually try to sleep now, I have service in ten hours and Chef Antoine will kill me if I'm slow on the line
You did the thing. That's the hard part. Now let your brain stop spinning and get some rest — you'll need your hands steady tomorrow. Good luck, and I hope Copenhagen comes calling.
